summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the Flask application accepts arbitrarily large request bodies without any size limit. A 5MB POST body was accepted with HTTP 202, proving that MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is not configured. A properly configured Flask application would reject this with HTTP 413 (Request Entity Too Large).
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should demonstrate that the Flask application accepts a 5MB request body with HTTP 202 instead of rejecting it with HTTP 413 (Request Entity Too Large). This proves MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is not set, enabling unbounded memory consumption and denial of service.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the reproduction image, started a Flask app mirroring the import endpoint pattern from changedetection.io, confirmed MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is None (unlimited), sent a normal 3-URL request (accepted with HTTP 202), then sent a 5MB request body containing 43,329 URLs which was also accepted with HTTP 202. The server read the entire 5,242,809 bytes into memory without any limit.
verification_method: The exploit script verifies that Flask's MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is None, then sends two requests: a normal-sized request and a 5MB request. Both are accepted with HTTP 202, confirming no body size limit exists. A protected Flask app would return HTTP 413 for the large request if MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH were configured.
evidence_produced_by_run: Flask MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH confirmed as None (unlimited), matching flask_app.py:61 which never sets this configuration.
Normal request (66 bytes, 3 URLs) accepted with HTTP 202.
Large request (5,242,809 bytes / 5.0 MB, 43,329 URLs) accepted with HTTP 202, body_size confirmed as 5,242,809 bytes read into memory.
The server accepted both requests identically, proving no request body size limit exists.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned ref, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
